coredata.dc:title: A fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymer using aptamer as a functional monomer for sensing of kanamycin
coredata.dc:creator: Geng, Yuanyuan
Guo, Manli
Tan, Jiean
Huang, Shuyi
Tang, Youwen
Tan, Lei
Liang, Yong
coredata.dc:description: This paper describes an alternative strategy for fabricating a fluorescent aptamer functionalized molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) for highly specific sensing of kanamycin. The technique provides surface imprinting in aqueous solutions using CdSe quantum dots as supports, thiols modified aptamer and methacrylic acid as functional monomers, and kanamycin as a template. The MIP would function utilizing double recognition of the aptamer and imprinted cavities for fluorescent sensing of kanamycin. The “thiol-ene” click reaction was utilized to fix the aptamer into polymer matrix, and the click chemistry used was highly efficient under mild condition and environmentally friendly. Experimental results indicated that there was a synergistic interaction between the aptamer and MAA, which improved the recognition ability of MIP toward kanamycin. The MIP showed an imprinting factor of 3.51 for kanamycin, excellent analog selectivity, and strong resistance toward ions interference. The fluorescence intensity of the MIP enhanced gradually with increase in the concentration of kanamycin, and exhibited good linear correlation within the concentration of 0.05–10.0 μg/mL with a detection limit of 0.013 μg/mL. This fluorescent MIP was further applied to quickly, sensitively and simply sensing of kanamycin in food, water and biological samples which yielded satisfied results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecularly imprinted polymer
Aptamer
Quantum dots
Click reaction
Kanamycin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carboxyl and allyl amine modified CdSe QDs
Preparation of the fluorescent aptamer functionalized molecularly imprinted polymer
Preparation of real samples and fluorescent measurements
Preparation, optimization and characterization of the fluorescent aptamer functionalized molecularly imprinted polymer
Time response of MIP composites for fluorescence detection of kanamycin
Effect of pH and temperature on fluorescence detection of kanamycin
Fluorescence sensing of kanamycin using MIP
Selectivity and reproducibility the MIP composites
Using MIP fluorescence sensor for selective detection of kanamycin in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the preparation and recognition process for the fluorescent aptamer functionalized molecularly imprinted polymer.
Effects of different molar ratios of template and functional monomers on the synthesis of the MIP and NIP.
(A) FT-IR spectra of (a) CdSe QDs, (b) CdSe QDs-AA, (c) the MIP; (B) The particle size of CdSe QDs.
(A) TEM image of CdSe QDs, the insets are SAED pattern and HRTEM image, (B) TEM image of CdSe QDs-AA, (C) TEM images of MIP, (D) XRD pattern of CdSe QDs and MIP.

The effect of pH on fluorescence intensity of the MIP and NIP after adding kanamycin (10.0 μg/mL) using the buffer solutions and concentrations: pH 5 ∼ 6: C6H8O7-Na3C6H5O7 (0.01 mol/L); pH 7–9: NaH2PO4-Na2HPO4 (0.01 mol/L); pH 10: Na2CO3-NaHCO3 (0.01 mol/L).
The effect of temperature on fluorescence intensity of the MIP and NIP in detection of kanamycin (10.0 μg/mL).
Fluorescence spectrum of the MIP with different concentrations of kanamycin ranging from 0.05-10.0 μg/mL, and the insets are the kanamycin concentration calibration curves.
Recovery for the determination of kanamycin in water, milk and urine samples.
The summary of different methods for the detection of kanamycin.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: SPE-CE: Solid-phase extraction and capillary electrophoresis.

head.ce:title: A fluorescent molecularly imprinted polymer using aptamer as a functional monomer for sensing of kanamycin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environment, South China Normal University, 510006, Guangzhou, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention, 510440, Guangzhou, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper describes an alternative strategy for fabricating a fluorescent aptamer functionalized molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) for highly specific sensing of kanamycin. The technique provides surface imprinting in aqueous solutions using CdSe quantum dots as supports, thiols modified aptamer and methacrylic acid as functional monomers, and kanamycin as a template. The MIP would function utilizing double recognition of the aptamer and imprinted cavities for fluorescent sensing of kanamycin. The “thiol-ene” click reaction was utilized to fix the aptamer into polymer matrix, and the click chemistry used was highly efficient under mild condition and environmentally friendly. Experimental results indicated that there was a synergistic interaction between the aptamer and MAA, which improved the recognition ability of MIP toward kanamycin. The MIP showed an imprinting factor of 3.51 for kanamycin, excellent analog selectivity, and strong resistance toward ions interference. The fluorescence intensity of the MIP enhanced gradually with increase in the concentration of kanamycin, and exhibited good linear correlation within the concentration of 0.05–10.0 μg/mL with a detection limit of 0.013 μg/mL. This fluorescent MIP was further applied to quickly, sensitively and simply sensing of kanamycin in food, water and biological samples which yielded satisfied results.

ce:section.ce:para: Aminoglycoside is a group of antibacterial antibiotics derived from species of Streptomyces or produced synthetically, and is widely used in the treatment of infections caused by Gram-negative bacteria [1,2]. They contains amino sugars in glycoside linkage which interfering with the synthesis of bacterial proteins [3]. Although they play an elective role in the treatment and prevention of infections, close clinical supervision is required because of its potential toxicity and adverse side effects to auditory, nerve and kidney renal tubules [4–6]. In addition, its residual in water and food may cause resistant bacteria, environmental contamination and food pollution, and eventually bringing potential risk to human health [7,8]. It is imperative to develop a simple, rapid, sensitive and selective technique to detect kanamycin in environmental water. A variety of methods have been developed for the detection of kanamycin, a typical aminoglycoside, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), high performance liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC–MS/MS), optical and electrochemical sensing [9–12]. Much progress has been made in the field of antibiotic detection using HPLC–MS/MS. However they are required a complex sample preparation step, and the instrument used is expensive for situations where a rapid response is needed [13].

Molecular recognition is well-known to play a vital role in highly selective detection by biosensors. Traditional molecular recognition elements including ligand-receptor, substrate-enzyme and antigen-antibody were widely utilized in the past few decades [14–16]. Although these recognition elements possess high binding affinity and specificity, they have certain disadvantages which cannot be neglected such as expensive cost, hard availability, poor stability and easy denaturation. Therefore, it is necessary to establish some alternatives to eliminate these restrictions. In recent years, a variety of attempts have been made by chemical researchers [17–20]. Molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP), is one of the most suitable substitutes, and has received wide-spread attention in purification and separation [21–24], chemo/biosensors [25–27], and drug delivery [28–30]. Owning tailor-made binding sites complementary to the template molecules in shape, size and functional groups, MIP have the unique ability of recognizing the template with high specificity and selectivity. In addition, compared to other recognition elements, artificial synthesis endows the MIP with higher physical stability, more remarkable robustness, lower cost and more times of cyclic utilization [31].

Aptamers have attracted increasing interest as another promising alternative in these years. In general, aptamers are short, single-stranded, functional DNA or RNA molecules selected from random-sequence nucleic acid combinatorial libraries via a procedure called systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) [32–34]. Aptamers have certain merits, such as they are nontoxic, chemically stable, small in size, inexpensive, easily modifiable, lack of immunogenicity and can bind nearly any target from small molecules [35] to protein [36] even microorganisms [37] and cells [38] with high affinity and specificity [39]. In general, flexibility and convenience in the design of aptamer sequences make them appealing biological molecular recognition elements [40,41].

Considering the remarkable characteristics of the above two molecular recognition elements, a novel recognition tool was developed by combining MIP with aptamers. Wei Bai et al. successfully prepared an imprinted hydrogel and applied it to detect protein [42] via volume response and sense virus [43] based on laser diffraction. Afterward, a variety of newer attempts were made to use MIP and aptamers composites in different applications including microgravimetric analysis using a quartz crystal microbalance [44], electrochemical strategy [45], isothermal titration calorimetry [46], a probe based on up-conversion nanoparticles [47], recognition system based on electrogenerated chemiluminescence energy transfer [48], signal cascade strategy based on gold nanoparticle-decorated polymer [49] and probe grafted onto a MWCNTs-Chit nanocomposite [50].

In the current paper, we have proposed a novel enhanced fluorescence sensing material through integration of highly specific aptamers with very selective molecular imprinting technique to recognize kanamycin. Because of the unique photophysical properties of quantum dots (QDs) which are strongly luminescent, extremely stable and wonderfully biocompatible, QDs based fluorescence sensors have been widely used in analysis [51,52]. We used vinyl modified CdSe QDs by coupling with allyl amine (AA) as the polymerizable support, and aptamers as biologically affiliative monomers, methacrylic acid (MAA) as chemical monomers based on the electrostatic interaction between MAA and template in a neutral environment, so that joining them the extra binding sites can be utilized after the template and aptamers bond as much as possible to enhance the recognition ability of the imprinted cavities toward the template. The aptamers were embedded into the polymers through “thiol-ene” click reaction. The MIP composite was used as a novel fluorescent sensing material making it high selective, sensitive and rapid detection of kanamycin in food, water and biological samples.

All chemicals were of analytical grade. CdCl2·5H2O and selenium powder were purchased from Aladdin (shanghai, China). 3-Mercapto propionic acid, methacrylic acid (MAA) and N, N'-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAAM) were obtained from Macklin (shanghai, China). Ammonium persulfate (APS) was received from Tianjin Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Allyl amine (AA) and N, N, N', N'-tetramethylethylethylenediamine (TEMED) were obtained from Shandong Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). Kanamycin, streptomycin, gentamicin, erythromycin, nucleic acid stain and the aptamer of kanamycin (5-SH-C6-TGGGGGTTGAGGCTAAGCCGA-C6-SH-3) were purchased from Sangon Biotechnology Inc. (shanghai, China). Other chemicals were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Company and used without further purification. All solutions were prepared with double deionized water (DDW).

The morphology and microstructure of CdSe QDs and MIP were studied using high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a JEM-4100HR (JEOL, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) on a JEM-2100HR (JEOL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra (4000–400 cm-1) in KBr were recorded on a Nicolet 6700 FT-IR Spectrometer (Foster City, CA, USA). Potential and particle size distribution were obtained from a MAL1150372 Laser particle size analyzer (Malvern, England). UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV–vis 1700 Spectrophotometer. The fluorescence spectra were collected from an F-4600 Hitachi spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained from a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carboxyl and allyl amine modified CdSe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of the 3-mercapto propionic acid modified CdSe QDs used in the present study was done following a reported method [53,54] with some modification. Specifically, a precursor solution of NaHSe was first prepared by dissolving Selenium powder (2.3 g, 29 mmol) and excess sodium borohydride (2.3 g, 61 mmol) into 25 mL ultrapure water under magnetic stirring at room temperature. A vigorous reaction started with considerable foaming due to hydrogen evolution in which the selenium powder was totally consumed in less than 5 min. The resulting colorless solution of NaHSe was further used without any further treatment. Second, 100 mL aqueous solution containing CdCl2·2.5H2O (0.228 g, 1 mmol) was made in a 250 mL round bottom flask. Next, 3-mercapto propionic acid (97.5 μL, 1.1 mmol) was added slowly into the above aqueous solution with continuous stirring. On adjusting the pH of the solution to 11 by adding freshly prepared 1 M NaOH solution drop by drop, the solution became turbid white. Third, a fraction of the freshly prepared NaHSe solution was added to the above mixture rapidly under nitrogen and intense magnetic stirring, followed by reflux of the mixture was refluxed at 100 °C for 2.5 h to promote the growth of the alloyed quantum dots. Finally, the carboxyl modified CdSe QDs was centrifuged by adding isopropyl alcohol. Aafter several washings, the purified CdSe QDs solution was stored in dark at 4 °C. Solid CdSe QDs was obtained by removing the solvent from CdSe QD solution under vacuum at room temperature.

A published report [55] was followed to couple allyl amine (AA) to CdSe QDs. The procedure is as follows. To a 25 mL round bottomed flask, were added 10 mL of CdSe QDs, 1 mL EDC (0.10 M) and 1 mL NHS (0.10 M), followed by stirring at 40 °C for 15 min to activate the carboxyl groups on the surface of CdSe QDs. Next, 10 μL AA was added into the mixture and kept stirring for 2 h to initiate coupling with the CdSe QDs via dehydration. The resulting mixture was centrifuged to obtain an orange precipitate which was washed a few times with isopropyl alcohol. The purified CdSe QDs-AA solution was stored in dark at 4 °C. Solid CdSe QDs-AA was obtained by drying in vacuum at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent aptamer functionalized molecularly imprinted polymer
ce:section.ce:para: There are two steps in the fabrication of hybrid aptamer-molecular imprinted polymer sensing material. First, MAA and thiol modified aptamer were added to 0.5 mL CdSe QDs-AA solution containing kanamycin, and the mixture was incubated for 2 h at 37 °C with slow stirring to form binding complexes. Second, the crosser linker MBAA was added. Before initiating any polymerization reaction, the mixture was purged with nitrogen to ensure an oxygen-free atmosphere. The polymerization was then triggered by adding the initiator APS and TEMED. The final reaction volume was kept at 1 mL utilizing phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4). The reaction was carried out at 40 °C in water bath for 6 h with appropriate stirring. The non-imprinted polymer (NIP) was synthesized in an identical manner but in absence of the template. After the reaction ceased, NIP and MIP composites were collected by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 10 min and washed with ultrapure water to remove any excess monomer and oligomer. The MIP composite was obtained after repeated washing with 0.1% acetic acid solution/acetonitrile (v/v, 2/8) until no template was detected by high-performance liquid chromatography with UV detector.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of real samples and fluorescent measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water and lake water were collected from our laboratory and Lake of Inkstone (Guangzhou, China) respectively. Yantang milk from a local supermarket was adopted for the real milk samples, and the urine samples was collected from pigs in the Institute of animal husbandry, Guangdong Academy of Agricultural Sciences. The environmental waste water samples were collected from the pig farms of Yujin and Hengcang in the city of Qingyuan (Guangdong, China) which belong to Dawnrays group. Prior use, the farms waste water samples were centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 20 min to remove all impurities, and 0.45 μm syringe filters were used to filter these waste water examples. Kanamycin standard solution was spiked into tap water and lake water directly, the 100-fold diluted milk and urine and 50-fold diluted actual farms waste water samples with phosphate buffer solution to prepare different concentrations of kanamycin. Standard addition method was adopted for subsequent detection with the hybrid probe MIP.

As for the fluorescence spectrophotometer, the slit widths were set to 10 nm for excitation as well as emission. At the same time, the photomultiplier tube voltage was kept at 400 V and the excitation wavelength at 365 nm. Before measurement, a series of standard solutions of kanamycin for calibration was prepared by dissolving appropriate amount of kanamycin in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) and stored at 4 °C. Then, 0.2 mL of NIP or MIP solution, 3 mL PBS and different concentrations of kanamycin (0.8 mL) were sequentially added to a 5 mL calibrated test tube.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation, optimization and characterization of the fluorescent aptamer functionalized molecularly imprinted polymer
ce:section.ce:para: Aptamers are short single-stranded oligonucleotides that capable of binding with high affinity and selectivity to a variety of targets, ranging from small molecules to proteins [56]. The introduction of the aptamer monomer was performed by modifying the aptamer with sulfhydryl. Combining the inherent molecular recognition ability of molecular imprinting technology and the affinity binding ability of aptamer for kanamycin, fluorescent MIP with double recognition elements was prepared. The MIP integrates versatile aptamer monomers inside their structure, the aptamer acts as the recognition part of a molecularly imprinted polymer. As shown in Fig. 1 , we designed a new and simple protocol which combined dual thiol modified aptamers with molecularly imprinted polymer on the surface of quantum dots to generate a double recognition fluorescence sensing system using “thiol-ene” click reaction. Compared to the universal method for surface molecular imprinting via sol-gel procedure, our method did not require functionalization of QDs with complicated silane reagents. In addition, the click reaction is highly efficient even in mild condition and easy to operate. The general scheme for the synthetic procedure is shown in Fig. 1. CdSe QDs were used to provide the sensing signal for composite materials. The formation of the MIP layer around CdSe QDs mainly relied on the reactive vinyl groups on the surface of CdSe QDs. In the spectrogram, when the CdSe QDs coupled with AA, its fluorescence intensity decreased to about 60% of that of the original CdSe QDs, and the fluorescence emission wavelength underwent a red shift by about 10 nm. After the polymerization process and template elution, the intensity of MIP composites was very similar to the intensity of NIP composites.

The molar ratio of the template and the monomers played an essential role in the specific recognition of MIP. It was necessary to carry out the optimization experiments. Considering the ratio of identification between the template and aptamers to be 1:1, we fixed the molar ratio of the template to aptamers also as 1:1 during the preparation of the MIP. Therefore, and only the molar ratio of MAA for NIP and that of the template and MAA for the MIP were optimized (Fig. 2 ). The ability of specific recognition was evaluated from the imprinting factor (IF):
IF = ΔMIP/ΔNIP
where ΔMIP and ΔNIP were the enhanced fluorescence intensity of MIP composites and NIP composites after rebinding the template. When the selected monomer was just the aptamer during the synthesis of MIP and NIP, the IF was 1.97. When the monomer was only MAA, the IF was only 1.31. However, when the aptamers and MAA were the monomers, the IF reached a maximum of 3.51 when the molar ratio of template, aptamers and MAA was 1:1:5. This IF of 3.51 was larger than 1.97, or 1.31 or even the sum of two. The result of the optimization experiments confirmed that there existed synergistic effect between the aptamers and MAA during the synthesis of MIP. The results demonstrated that the high affinity of the aptamers and imprinted cavities contributed to the improvement in specific recognition of the MIP. Therefore, 1:1:5 was chosen as the optimal molar ratio of the template, aptamers and MAA to synthesize MIP for our subsequent experiments.

The FT-IR spectrum of CdSe QDs, CdSe QDs-AA and MIP were performed to study the chemical modifications occurred in each procedure (Fig. 3 A). The CdSe QDs displayed two peaks at 3207 cm−1 and 1647 cm−1, which corresponded to the stretching vibration of OH and CO and indicated the presence of carboxylic group. Additionally, a strong but narrow peak at 1632 cm−1 was assigned to the stretching vibration of terminal CC, which indicated that the CdSe QDs were successfully functionalized via modification of the vinyl groups to form CdSe QDs-AA. The FT-IR spectra of MIP showed an absorption peak at 2921 cm−1, which could be ascribed to the stretching vibration of CH. This peak suggested that monomer MAA and cross-linker MBAAM were polymerized in the MIP layer. One thing to note is that the stretching vibration of CO at 1637 cm−1 and the in-plane bending vibration of NH at 1523 cm−1 in MIP might have overlapped with that in the CdSe QDs-AA. This is because both of them had the secondary amide bond. The former was also derived from the cross-linker MBAAM of polymerization except the CdSe QDs-AA itself had contained from the coupling reaction. Therefore, a close resemblance between the absorption peaks of MIP and CdSe QDs-AA is justified. As shown in Fig. S1, no significant difference has been observed among the NIP before washing, the MIP before washing, the NIP after washing, and the MIP after washing.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) studies were carried out to understand the size and morphology of the CdSe QDs and MIP. Fig. 4 A shows that the CdSe QDs appeared spherical in shape and almost uniform in size. The estimated average particle size was approximately 3.2 nm, which was very close to that calculated from the size distribution (Fig. 3B). The insets exhibit a high-resolution TEM micrograph of a single CdSe QD. The lattice fringes were clearly visible, demonstrating high crystallinity. Selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns also revealed that the lattice structure of the CdSe QDs was cubic zinc blende, which was totally consistent with the results of the XRD. In Fig. 4B, compared to CdSe QDs, CdSe QDs-AA did not show many changes. On the other hand, the MIP also had spherical nanostructure but a much larger diameter about 25 nm, as shown in Fig. 4C. Such a large diameter was mainly due to the aggregation of the particles during polymerization and the polymer shell around the CdSe QDs. Fig. 4D shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the synthesized CdSe QDs and MIP. Strong diffraction peaks of the CdSe QDs in the 2-theta range of 10−70° can be observed which corresponded to a cubic zinc blende structure with peaks for (111), (220), and (311). More importantly, the XRD pattern of MIP was the same as that of the CdSe QDs, which proved the polymerization procedure had no influence on the structure of the CdSe QDs.

In order to examine whether the aptamers have anchored within the MIP, we quantified the bonded aptamers in the polymer by estimating the unbonded aptamers in the supernatant (Fig. S2 and Table S1). A very small amount of aptamers were detected in the supernatant of prepared MIP and NIP composites. The estimated values were 5.9% and 7.1% of the total amount of aptamers added, respectively, thereby indicating that most of the aptamers up to 94% and 93% were incorporated into the polymers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Time response of MIP composites for fluorescence detection of kanamycin
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. S3 shows the time response of MIP in detecting kanamycin. As soon as kanamycin was added to the NIP composite, enhancement in fluorescence intensity occurred which then remained stable after about 15 min. The MIP composites also showed the same fluorescence enhancement phenomena. However, it took more time, about 25 min, to reach equilibrium state for the adsorption and desorption, and remained stable at a much higher fluorescence intensity. These results suggested that the equilibrium adsorption of NIP was faster than that of the MIP. The reason was the kanamycin took more time to penetrate into the double recognized specific imprinted caves which were complementary to the template in space, size and configuration. Therefore, we selected 25 min as our optimal response time for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and temperature on fluorescence detection of kanamycin
Fluorescence sensing of kanamycin using MIP
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the spectroscopic response of the MIP toward kanamycin, the fluorescence signal of the composite material at different concentrations of kanamycin in the range 0.05–10.0 μg/mL was recorded. Fig. 7 shows that the fluorescence intensity of the MIP increased gradually with increasing concentration of the template kanamycin. The reason for such enhanced fluorescence was mainly due to the adsorptive affinity between the imprinted cavities of MIP and the template kanamycin. Furthermore, the enhanced fluorescence intensity was proportional to the logarithm of the concentration of kanamycin. As shown in the inset of Fig. 7, the calibration plots of (F-F0) versus ln(C·102) of MIP presented a good linear relationship with correlation coefficient of 0.998 when the concentration of kanamycin was within the range 0.05–10.0 μg/mL. The limit of detection was calculated from 3σ/S ratio, where σ was the standard deviation of the blank signal (for ten replicates) and S was the slope of the linear calibration. The detection limit calculated for kanamycin was 0.013 μg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and reproducibility the MIP composites
ce:section.ce:para:
Possessing high selectivity is always desirable for any sensor. Hence, selectivity of the newly developed composites was also evaluated. In this regard, some analogs were selected as control, viz. amikacin, streptomycin, gentamicin and erythromycin (Fig. S4). Detection was performed at concentration of 17.2 μmol/L of kanamycin, while concentrations of the other analogs were same as that of the template. The selectivity factor (γ) was calculated by the equation:
γ MIP (γNIP) = Δ F template/Δ F analog
where ΔF template and ΔF analog are the enhanced fluorescence intensity of MIP or NIP after rebinding of the analytes respectively.

As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of MIP composite enhanced effectively up to 3.3 times by adding kanamycin while the intensity was much lower for others. Among the analogs, the best selectivity factor of 3.92 was observed for gentamicin because its structure was the closest to that of the template. The selectivity factor for streptomycin and erythromycin were 5.42 and 13.0, respectively, because their structures were less similar to that of the template and their sizes did not match imprinted cavities. In comparison to MIP, the NIP showed a much lower selectivity factor. These results indicated the MIP material had good selectivity and specificity toward kanamycin. One thing to note is that the NIP showed a low selectivity due to the specificity of aptamers.

The effect of relevant ions in biochemical samples on the efficiency in kanamycin analysis was further explored. As shown in Table S2, the changes in fluorescence intensities in presence of different ions were less than ± 5% which suggested that they have negligible effects on the analysis of kanamycin.

The reproducibility experiment was also performed (Fig. S5). Following the same procedure, we fabricated five batches of imprinted polymers to sense kanamycin at concentration of 10.0 μg/mL. The imprinted polymers showed good reproducibility, and the RSD value was 2.4%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Using MIP fluorescence sensor for selective detection of kanamycin in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing of kanamycin in real samples was carried out in order to investigate the practical application of this new sensor. The results of standard addition method experiment are shown in Table 1 . As shown in Table 1, different concentrations of kanamycin including 0.2, 1.0 and 4.0 μg/mL were added into tap water, lake water, milk and urine, and the recoveries of kanamycin range from 85.3% to 116% with the RSD of less than 5.0%. These analysis results indicated that the new sensing method shows potential in directly detecting kanamycin in real samples.

Several methods have been reported in literature for detection of kanamycin in real samples, and their key performances are summered in Table 2. As shown in Table 2 , a level of analytical performance has been achieved by the current method. The detection limit and linear range of this method is comparable or superior to those obtained by previous works. Moreover, without complex sample preparation, this method has been successfully applied to the detection of kanamycin in food, water and biological samples with satisfied recoveries.

In conclusion, a fluorescent aptamer functionalized molecularly imprinted polymer for recognition of kanamycin was developed in the present work. Compared to fluorescence sensing material prepared only by aptamers or MAA, the present double recognition system of aptamers and imprinted cavities possessed higher specificity and selectivity in recognition. Rapid, simple and accurate analysis of kanamycin can be realized with the aptamer functionalized molecularly imprinted polymer. The application of “thiol-ene” click reaction made the polymerization procedure more convenient and efficient. In addition, this fluorescent sensing system showed strong resistance to ion interference and was successfully employed to food, water and biologcial samples and indicated its potential in practical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21505026, 21275057, and 51478196) and the Science and Technology Planning Project of Guangdong Province (2017A020212003 and 2016A020215020).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.04.065.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aminoglycoside antibiotics: old drugs and new therapeutic approaches
Current use for old antibacterial agents: polymyxins, rifamycins, and aminoglycosides
New insights into the mechanism of aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity: an integrative point of view
Aminoglycoside antibiotics induce bacterial biofilm formation
Development of liquid chromatographic method for the analysis of kanamycin residues in varicella vaccine using phenylisocyanate as a derivatization reagent
Determination of microbial transglutaminase in meat and meat products
A novel electrochemical aptasensor for ultrasensitive detection of kanamycin based on MWCNTs-HMIMPF 6 and nanoporous PtTi alloy
A novel signal amplification strategy of an electrochemical aptasensor for kanamycin, based on thionine functionalized graphene and hierarchical nanoporous PtCu
A review of analytical methods for the determination of aminoglycoside and macrolide residues in food matrices
Immuno-PCR: very sensitive antigen detection by means of specific antibody-DNA conjugates
An enzyme-Based E-DNA sensor for sequence-specific detection of femtomolar DNA targets
Micropatterned immobilization of a G protein-coupled receptor and direct detection of G protein activation
Protein recognition by a surface imprinted colloidal array
An engineered high affinity Fbs1 carbohydrate binding protein for selective capture of N-glycans and N-glycopeptides
Lateral flow assay-based bacterial detection using engineered cell wall binding domains of a phage endolysin
Ultrasensitive detection of aflatoxin B 1 by SERS aptasensor based on exonuclease-assisted recycling amplification
Controlled synthesis of pentachlorophenol-imprinted polymers on the surface of magnetic graphene oxide for highly selective adsorption
Efficient removal of lead from highly acidic wastewater by periodic ion imprinted mesoporous SBA-15 organosilica combining metal coordination and co-condensation
Selective and simultaneous removal of dibenzothiophene and 4-methyldibenzothiophene using double-template molecularly imprinted polymers on the surface of magnetic mesoporous silica
Highly ordered metal ion imprinted mesoporous silica particles exhibiting specific recognition and fast adsorption kinetics
Molecularly imprinted polymer on graphene surface for selective and sensitive electrochemical sensing imidacloprid
Fluorescent molecularly imprinted membranes as biosensor for the detection of target protein
Synthesis and characterization of fluorescence molecularly imprinted polymers as sensor for highly sensitive detection of dibutyl phthalate from tap water samples
Molecular imprinted polymers as drug delivery vehicles
Ligand replacement approach to Raman-responded molecularly imprinted monolayer for rapid determination of penicilloic acid in penicillin
Molecularly imprinted polymer-based plasmonic immunosandwich assay for fast and ultrasensitive determination of trace glycoproteins in complex samples
Systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment: RNA ligands to bacteriophage T4 DNA polymerase
In vitro selection of RNA moleculars that bind specific ligands
Selection of single-stranded DNA molecules that bind and inhibit human thrombin
Fluoromodules consisting of a promiscuous RNA aptamer and red or blue fluorogenic cyanine dyes: selection, characterization, and bioimaging
Single-molecule detection of protein efflux from microorganisms using fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube sensor arrays
Integrating recognition elements with nanomaterials for bacteria sensing
Enhanced and differential capture of circulating tumor cells from lung cancer patients by microfl uidic assays using aptamer cocktail
Aptamer-integrated DNA nanostructures for biosensing, bioimaging and cancer therapy
Rapid and selective detection of pathogenic bacteria in bloodstream infections with aptamer-based recognition
Highly sensitive detection of bisphenol a by NanoAptamer assay with truncated aptamer
Macromolecular amplification of binding response in superaptamer hydrogels
A double-imprinted diffraction-grating sensor based on a virus-responsive super-aptamer hydrogel derived from an impure extract
Generation of novel hybrid aptamer-molecularly imprinted polymeric nanoparticles
Aptamer-MIP hybrid receptor for highly sensitive electrochemical detection of prostate specific antigen
Molecularly imprinted polymers with DNA aptamer fragments as macromonomers
Novel hybrid probe based on double recognition of aptamer-molecularly imprinted polymer grafted on upconversion nanoparticles for enrofloxacin sensing
Aptamer-molecularly imprinted sensor base on electrogenerated chemiluminescence energy transfer for detection of lincomycin
A nanoparticle-decorated biomolecule-responsive polymer enables robust signaling cascade for biosensing
A nanohybrid probe based on double recognition of an aptamer MIP grafted onto a MWCNTs-chit nanocomposite for sensing hepatitis C virus core antigen
CdSe quantum dot-encapsulated molecularly imprinted mesoporous silica particles for fluorescent sensing of bisphenol A
Large-scale fabrication of heavy doped carbon quantum dots with tunable-photoluminescence and sensitive fluorescence detection
The influence of capping thioalkyl acid on the growth and photoluminescence efficiency of CdTe and CdSe quantum dots
Thiol-capping of CdTe nanocrystals: an alternative to organometallic synthetic routes
Selective fluorescent sensing of α-amanitin in serum using carbon quantum dots-embedded specificity determinant imprinted polymers
Label-free detection of kanamycin based on the aptamer-functionalized conducting polymer/gold nanocomposite
A kanamycin sensor based on an electrosynthesized molecularly imprinted poly-o-phenylenediamine film on a single-walled carbon nanohorn modified glassy carbon electrode
Determination of kanamycin in serum by solid-phase extraction, pre-capillary derivatization and capillary electrophoresis
Validated spectrophotometric methods for determination of certain aminoglycosides in pharmaceutical formulations
Colorimetric aptasensor using unmodified gold nanoparticles for homogeneous multiplex detection
Label-free detection of kanamycin using aptamer-based cantilever array sensor
An oligonucleotide-based switch-on luminescent probe for the detection of kanamycin in aqueous solution

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Yuanyuan Geng is studying for master degree at School of Chemistry and Envriomental, South China Normal University.
Ganli Guo is a is an assosicate Professor at School of Chemistry and Envriomental, South China Normal University. Her main research fields are biosensing and environmental analysis.
Jiean Tan is studying for master degree at School of Chemistry and Envriomental, South China Normal University.
Shuyi Huang is studying for master degree at School of Chemistry and Envriomental, South China Normal University.
Youwen Tang is a Professor at School of Chemistry and Envriomental, South China Normal University. His main research interests are sample preparation, biosensing and environmental analysis.
Lei Tan is a researcher (technician) at Guangzhou Center for Disease Control and Prevention, Guangzhou China. His main research interests are sample preparation, biosensing and HPLC–MS/MS analysis.
Yong Liang is a Professor at School of Chemistry and Envriomental, South China Normal University. His main research interests are sample preparation, biosensing and environmental analysis.
